title: Burnham pauses early prison release scheme
description: Andy Burnham has announced he is pausing a scheme which would have seen thousands of prisoners in England and Wales eligible to be released early.It c
body: Andy Burnham has announced he is pausing a scheme which would have seen thousands of prisoners in England and Wales eligible to be released early.It came after criticism of the scheme from survivors and victims' families, including the widow of PC Andrew Harper who described the possibility of his killers' early release as "deplorable".The Sentencing Act, which was due to come into effect in September, would have seen some offenders let out at varying stages in a bid to ease prison overcrowding.The new Justice Secretary Alex Norris said the pause aimed to "give victims and survivors certainty" and the government wanted to make sure it got "the implementation of this policy right".He added a review would also look at the information given to survivors and victims' families as part of the early release process.Burnham earlier said: "No prisoners will be let out under this policy until we have conducted an urgent review and done everything we can to minimise risk to the public."He added: "Where changes are needed, we will not hesitate to make them."Under the act, some offenders would have been let out after serving a third of their sentence, rather than half. The rest of their sentence would be spent in the community, where they would be monitored and could be returned to prison if they offended again. More serious offenders would have been released after serving half of their sentence, rather than two-thirds.The policy was brought in to ease overcrowding in the prison system but has been criticised by opposition parties, as well as victims and their families.The scheme would have seen around 700 prisoners released in early September, the BBC understands. The decision to pause the scheme came after criticism from the family of Thames Valley Police officer Andrew Harper, who was killed in Berkshire in 2019 when he was dragged by a car down a country road by three teenagers.Two of the killers of PC Harper, who were both sentenced to 13 years in prison, would have benefited from the scheme from June 2027, when the longest fixed sentences would have been added to the scheme.PC Harper's widow, Lissie, called the plans "deplorable" and said politicians were "making decisions from behind a desk that will have real and lasting consequences" for the families of victims.His mother, meanwhile, said her son had not been given "proper justice" and "every victim will be feeling the same" about the early prison release scheme.Thousands of letters had been sent to victims in recent weeks, warning them that the early release scheme was coming in September.Norris, who voted in support of the Sentencing Act under Starmer's government, later said the decision to pause was made after "listening to victims" and he hoped to provide them "certainty as soon as we can".He said: "The prime minister and I have heard from victims and survivors and from people across the country about their concerns about the implementation of some very important elements of the Sentencing Act."One element the government needed to get right was the information provided to victims before prisoners were released, Norris added. Norris refused to be drawn on why he did not raise concerns when the Sentencing Act was first introduced but said Burnham was "quite right" to review the scheme.Jade Belgrove, a survivor of sexual assault who has been campaigning against the early release scheme, said it was "such a relief that we've been heard".She told BBC News that when she received a letter informing her that her attacker might be released early "it was horrific".Belgrove said it even led her to question whether reporting the assault in the first place was worth it.Victims' Commissioner Claire Waxman welcomed the decision, calling the  original form of the early release scheme the "wrong course of action"."Thousands of victims have already been told the offenders who harmed them could be released early, leaving many living with renewed fear and uncertainty," she said. "Victims deserve certainty, and their safety and confidence must come first."Conservative Party leader Kemi Badenoch said she welcomed the pause but called for killers and rapists to be "permanently" exempt from any future version of the scheme. In a post on X, she said that the Tories have been "campaigning against this dangerous scheme from day one".Steve Gillan general secretary of prison officers' union POA said he understood concerns about "operational stability" but the government was correct to pause early releases "to ensure there is adequate public protection in place and that victims of crime are put first".But one prison officer told the BBC Burnham's decision was "bonkers" and warned prisons were close to full capacity. "We're super overcrowded - this isn't great news," they said."If we don't let people out in September we could be at a critical point come November where we're at full capacity."Another prison officer disagreed, praising the government for "putting public safety first".

schema.headline: Burnham pauses early prison release scheme

title: UK 'ready to defend itself' after Iran says bases are 'legitimate targets'
description: The British government has said it "stands ready 24/7 to defend itself" after Iran said UK bases used by the US were considered "legitimate targets".
body: The British government has said it "stands ready 24/7 to defend itself" after Iran said UK bases used by the US were considered "legitimate targets". The UK has allowed the US to launch "defensive" operations from British bases hosting American personnel and aircraft, while refusing to cooperate in offensive operations against Iran.Iran's foreign ministry said the UK was an "accomplice" in the US war, while the powerful Islamic Revolution Guards Corps (IRGC) military wing said any country which supports the US militarily "will be responsible for the consequences and repercussions".It came after the US carried out a 12th consecutive night of strikes against targets in Iran.While US President Donald Trump has repeatedly criticised the British government and former Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer for what he has deemed a lack of support for his war, Iran's comments on 23 July sought to paint the UK as a party to the campaign.In March, days into the US-Israel led war, permission was given to the US for it to use British bases including RAF Fairford in Gloucestershire and Diego Garcia in the Indian Ocean to target Iranian military infrastructure used to attack Gulf countries and vessels in the Strait of Hormuz.That policy has not been altered by new Prime Minister Andy Burnham, who was notified last week that a decision had been taken to extend the agreement with the US.In a statement published on the IRGC-linked Tasnim News Agency on 23 July, the group said American bombing missions had been launched from RAF Fairford two days earlier.The UK Ministry of Defence has not commented on whether the base was used by the US for operations against Iran this week.Responding to the Iranian threats, a UK government spokesperson said: "Our Armed Forces are ready to keep the United Kingdom safe from any kind of attacks, whether it's on our soil or from abroad. The UK stands ready 24/7 to defend itself."This includes through operating a layered approach to air and missile defence, provided by Royal Navy, British Army and Royal Air Force assets equipped with a range of advanced capabilities, working closely with our Nato allies."The UK's approach to the conflict remains the same - we are committed to defending our people, our interests and our allies, acting in accordance with international law and not getting drawn into the wider conflict."Days after the war began on 28 February, Iran's ambassador to London, Seyed Ali Mousavi, said Iran would have the "right to self-defence" if the UK joined the war, and said it should be "very careful" about taking part.On 19 March, Iran fired two ballistic missiles at the UK's Diego Garcia base in the Chagos Islands. One reportedly failed mid-flight, with the other shot down by a US airship.There were doubts then over whether Iran had missiles which were capable of reaching the archipelago, which is about 2,360 miles (3,600km) from Iran.The same month, Israel claimed Iran possessed missiles that could reach up to 4,000km (2,485 miles), which would theoretically mean they could reach the UK and mainland European countries. However, then Housing Secretary Steve Reed told BBC News at the time there was "no specific assessment that the Iranians are targeting the UK - or even could if they wanted to".Analysts have also raised doubts over the reliability and accuracy of Iran's missile arsenal, which the US says has been severely degraded by its strikes. Iran has launched strikes against several countries in the Gulf which house US military bases in recent months, while attacks against civilian vessels in the Strait of Hormuz have effectively closed the vital shipping route.

schema.headline: UK 'ready to defend itself' after Iran says bases are 'legitimate targets'

title: Italy manager's job: Azzurri approached Carlo Ancelotti before Pep Guardiola, says Paolo Maldini
description: Italy approached Carlo Ancelotti to become their national team manager before holding talks with Pep Guardiola. The Azzurri have been without a head c
body: Italy approached Carlo Ancelotti to become their national team manager before holding talks with Pep Guardiola. The Azzurri have been without a head coach since Gennaro Gattuso resigned in April following the country's failure to qualify for a third successive World Cup. Earlier this week, Italian Football Federation (FIGC) president Giovanni Malago said talks had been held with Guardiola, who left Manchester City this summer after 10 years, and "exceptions" could be made for his salary.But on Thursday, FIGC technical director Paolo Maldini said Italy first approached their former player Ancelotti, who is currently in charge of Brazil."We cannot hide the fact that we also spoke to Carlo before talking to Pep," said Maldini during a news conference. "Frankly, it seemed right to start with those we considered the best in the world." Ancelotti, a former team-mate of Maldini for both Italy and AC Milan, took charge of Brazil in 2025 and extended his contract in May until the 2030 World Cup.And despite a World Cup last-16 exit at the hands of Norway, the Brazilian Football Confederation confirmed the 67-year-old will remain in charge of the five-time world champions.

schema.headline: Italy manager's job: Azzurri approached Carlo Ancelotti before Pep Guardiola, says Paolo Maldini

title: Southport killer Axel Rudakubana moved from Belmarsh prison to Broadmoor psychiatric hospital
description: Axel Rudakubana, who murdered three children at a dance class in Southport, has been moved from prison to a secure psychiatric hospital.The Ministry o
body: Axel Rudakubana, who murdered three children at a dance class in Southport, has been moved from prison to a secure psychiatric hospital.The Ministry of Justice (MoJ) confirmed he was transferred from HMP Belmarsh to Broadmoor, the high-security psychiatric hospital in Berkshire.Rudakubana murdered Elsie Dot Stancombe, seven, Bebe King, six, and Alice da Silva Aguiar, nine, and injured eight other children and two adults in an attack at a Taylor Swift-themed dance class in July 2024.He was given a 52-year prison sentence in January 2025 but the judge was unable to impose a whole life order due to Rudakubana being 17 when he carried out the attack. He will likely never be released.The then Attorney-General Lord Hermer said Rudakubana's sentence was the "second-longest sentence imposed by the courts in English history".A spokesperson for the MoJ told PA: "The families of those murdered and injured in Southport will be in this country's heart forever and our thoughts remain with them."Any decision to transfer an offender is made following an independent, clinical assessment, under the Mental Health Act. Following rigorous security checks, the offender has been transferred to a high-security psychiatric unit, where he will remain until assessed fit to return to prison."Officials have approved this transfer, on behalf of Ministers. This transfer does not change his sentence – which remains at 52 years – and of which he will serve every single day."The cost of keeping Rudakubana in hospital is likely to be much higher in Broadmoor than a prison. According to the Centre for Mental Health the average secure hospital bed costs around £270,000 a year.The average cost of keeping a prisoner in Belmarsh is £97,740 according to figures for 2023.Broadmoor is the oldest of the UK's three high-security hospitals and has held some of the UK's most high-profile criminals.It was renovated in 2017 and the BBC obtained pictures from inside the hospital. They show that older prison-like cells were replaced by more spacious rooms, though still with locks on the doors.In May 2025 there was an investigation after Rudakubana, now 19, allegedly attacked a prison officer with boiling water.The Southport Public Inquiry has heard evidence about his past mental health conditions.A senior clinician at Alder Hey Children's Hospital described his "lack of empathy" combined with a "fascination with violence" and neurodiversity, which he said added up to a "really toxic mix".According to West London NHS Trust, Broadmoor specialises in "providing assessment, treatment and care in conditions of high security" for men and is one of only three high secure psychiatric hospitals in England and Wales.

schema.headline: Southport killer Axel Rudakubana moved from Belmarsh prison to Broadmoor psychiatric hospital

title: Saudis must recognise Israel for nuclear deal, says Trump
description: Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, US President Donald Trump, seen with his Saudi counterpart Mohammed bin Salman, says Saudi Arabia will not be
body: Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, US President Donald Trump, seen with his Saudi counterpart Mohammed bin Salman, says Saudi Arabia will not be able to enrich uranium under the dealByRobert GreenallUpdated 2 hours agoThe deal between the US and Saudi Arabia to help the Gulf kingdom develop nuclear energy is dependent on Riyadh recognising the State of Israel, President Donald Trump has said."The Civil Nuclear Deal (There will be no enrichment of material!)... which pertains only to non-military use... will be approved, but is totally subject to Saudi Arabia joining the very respected and successful Abraham Accords," Trump said on Truth Social.It was not immediately clear if the condition of joining the accords was stipulated in the text of the nuclear deal - which has not been released - or that Saudi Arabia had agreed to that condition. Saudi Arabia has not responded to Trump's remarks so far.White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said on Thursday that Trump had discussed the condition to Saudi leaders on numerous previous occasions."He has said if they don't join the Abraham Accords, the deal is off," she said. "So we'll continue to have those conversations with our Saudi counterparts moving forward."Trump's comments come come after criticism from experts that the US-Saudi deal announced on Wednesday could run the risk of future nuclear proliferation in an already unstable region. Some Republican and Democratic lawmakers expressed similar concerns, though Trump's Republican Party controls both the upper and lower chambers and opponents of the deal do not appear to have the votes needed to block it.According to the US Department of Energy, it includes a "peaceful nuclear co-operation agreement" and a "bilateral safeguards agreement". "Together, these two agreements lay the legal foundation for a decades-long, multi-billion-dollar partnership that advances several priority economic and strategic objectives, including nuclear nonproliferation," a statement from the department said.It said the agreement would be sent to the US Congress for review.  The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) said on Thursday that it was aware of US and Saudi plans for a "bilateral civil nuclear cooperation agreement and the plan to ask the IAEA to implement verification measures in relation to this"."We look forward to receiving the request for such verification and to working with the US and KSA [Kingdom of Saudi Arabia] in ensuring the implementation of those measures."During Trump's first term as US president, the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Bahrain signed the Abraham Accords, resulting in a historic normalisation of relations between Israel and two Arab nations - the first in decades.Sudan, Morocco and Kazakhstan have since also signed them, too.Saudi Arabia has previously said it will not do so without the establishment of a Palestinian state, but the White House said the deal would be off if Riyadh did not join the accords.Israel has welcomed Trump's statement.Posting on X, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's office said Saudi Arabia joining the accords "would be an historic leap forward for peace in the Middle East".The statement did not mention the nuclear deal. However, Israeli Economy Minister Nir Barkat said previously his country "can manage it" if Saudi Arabia had nuclear power for peaceful means.Others were more critical. Former Israeli defence minister Avigdor Lieberman, told the paper: "Every military nuclear program begins with a civilian one."Israel itself is widely believed to have nuclear weapons, but it neither confirms nor denies that this is the case.The US-Saudi deal is described as a "peaceful nuclear co-operation agreement" that will provide "great access for American companies in the Saudi nuclear energy programme" by the US energy department.The nuclear issue has been at the centre of the months-long US-Israeli conflict with Iran which has for years denied Western accusations that it aims to acquire nuclear weapons.Saudi Arabia's de facto ruler, Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, told CBS News in 2018 that his own nation was not planning to acquire a nuclear weapon, but that "without a doubt if Iran developed a nuclear bomb, we will follow suit as soon as possible".Earlier US media reports suggested the deal could allow Saudi Arabia to enrich uranium in the future - which can be used as fuel for power plants, but also potentially to make nuclear bombs. However, Trump's remarks appear to deny this.Rosemary Kelanic, the director of the Middle East programme at the US-based Defense Priorities think tank, said it would be "quite shocking" if the US were to allow Saudi Arabia uranium enrichment facilities."The US has never done that before. We have never helped another country enrich on their own soil," she said, adding that this was because "if you can produce your own nuclear fuel, you become a much bigger risk" for building nuclear weapons.

schema.headline: Saudis must recognise Israel for nuclear deal, says Trump

title: Senior ministers and aides set to work from No 10 North
description: Senior ministers and aides close to Andy Burnham are expecting to regularly work from No 10 North, which the prime minister has said will be the "domi
body: Senior ministers and aides close to Andy Burnham are expecting to regularly work from No 10 North, which the prime minister has said will be the "dominant driver of the UK's economy".Education Secretary Lucy Powell and Louise Haigh, who is overseeing No 10 North in her role as Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster, are planning to work there.The new branch of Downing Street is currently operating from Heron House in Manchester city centre, whilst a brownfield site is redeveloped. A blueprint for No 10 North produced by the think tank Re:State recommends a workforce of as many as 300 staff within 18 months.Burnham aims to base himself at No 10 North at least one day a week and is expected to start work there for the first time in coming days.He will be joined by his closest adviser, Kevin Lee, the director of his mayoral office, who will move to No 10 North after next week's Greater Manchester mayoral election.Lee is one of at least seven Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA) employees, including four senior managers, to have been recruited by Burnham and are already moving into No 10 North.Chief executive Caroline Simpson is moving to become Downing Street deputy chief of staff based at No 10 North, and directors John Wrathmell and Claire Norman will also move across.Some London-based civil servants have voiced concerns about the uncertainty surrounding the plans, with most details expected to be announced in the weeks ahead.In the weeks before Burnham became prime minister, the BBC has been told that senior officials were summoned to calls informing them that a significant programme to move staff out of London was being developed.Those who were on leave were told the plans would already be under way by the time they returned, according to a senior civil servant familiar with the discussions.Dave Penman, general secretary of the FDA union which represents senior civil servants, welcomed the changes to the structure of government but cautioned that relocation was a big decision for staff."Those considering a move out of London have the added concern that a return would be difficult given the disparity in house prices," he said, adding a major consideration would be "whether it feels like a permanent reorganisation or something that could change quickly with a future government".Burnham has told cabinet members No 10 North is not a gimmick in a bid to reassure critics – and civil servants whose careers may be reshaped by the plan – who say it still has to prove it can make a lasting difference.Dr Simon Kaye, author of the Re:State report told the BBC that No 10 North had to be big enough to outlast the Burnham administration."That means reaching a critical mass of people, establishing a genuine sense of shared purpose, and getting to a place where No 10 North feels different enough and vital enough that it can persist, no matter who the prime minister is," he said.While civil servants who spoke to the BBC largely welcomed the idea of a No 10 North, some doubted whether it would feel substantially different from existing regional government offices while the majority of ministers and political journalists, as well as many business leaders, continue to be based in London.Some civil servants based in northern England believe they should have been given the opportunity to apply for jobs at No 10 North before discussions began about potentially lucrative relocation packages for staff moving from London.One official, speaking anonymously due to the Civil Service Code, told the BBC: "I've built my career here and seen first-hand that the North is home to exceptional talent. "No 10 North needs to make the best use of all talent and create opportunities for people to build careers close to the communities they serve."Northern Powerhouse Partnership chief executive Henri Murison said the government had been moving civil servants out of London for several years, including to the new Darlington Economic Campus, adding: "This has seen a number of senior civil servants be based from here but only rarely ministers."

schema.headline: Senior ministers and aides set to work from No 10 North

title: Bruno Guimaraes transfer news: Arsenal set to bid £70m for Newcastle midfielder
description: Arsenal are set to make a £70m offer for Newcastle captain Bruno Guimaraes.Brazil international Guimaraes is a priority target for the Gunners, with t
body: Arsenal are set to make a £70m offer for Newcastle captain Bruno Guimaraes.Brazil international Guimaraes is a priority target for the Gunners, with the midfielder keen to join the Premier League champions.Newcastle are aware of the 28-year-old's preference to join Arsenal this summer.Talks over a potential move for Guimaraes are ongoing and are believed to have accelerated in recent days. The agreement of personal terms is not expected to be an issue, though it remains to be seen if Arsenal's £70m offer will meet Newcastle's valuation.Sources close to Newcastle insist the Magpies have no intention of selling Guimaraes this summer and that there has been no direct approach from the Gunners for their player.Arsenal are determined to strengthen their squad with high quality players this summer as they look to build on last season's title.The north London side were interested in signing Morgan Rogers, who has joined Chelsea for £117m, from Aston Villa – but refused to go higher than their £80m valuation of the player.It is understood Arsenal were given the opportunity to match Chelsea's offer for Rogers but declined.Similarly, Arsenal have a hardline maximum valuation of Guimaraes and are unwilling to overpay despite their desire to sign the midfielder.Arsenal also have a major interest in Villa defender Ezri Konsa and have enquired about the England international's availability.Konsa is Arsenal's first-choice defensive target.The Gunners also have Como's Jacobo Ramon on a list of defenders they would like to bring in, but it is understood clauses in the Spaniard's contract make a deal difficult.Having missed out on Rogers, the Gunners remain in the market for attacking reinforcements.Atletico Madrid forward Julian Alvarez is Arsenal's dream target, but the player's current desire to join Barcelona and the cost - which could exceed the British record fee of £125m set when Alexander Isak moved from Newcastle to Liverpool - pose obvious difficulties.Arsenal have also explored a move for Paris St-Germain winger Bradley Barcola.

schema.headline: Bruno Guimaraes transfer news: Arsenal set to bid £70m for Newcastle midfielder

title: Burnham tells Swinney independence is 'off limits' at Glasgow Commonwealth Games meeting
description: Image source, ReutersImage caption, Andy Burnham and John Swinney discussed inter-government relationsByCraig WilliamsBBC ScotlandPrime Minister Andy
body: Image source, ReutersImage caption, Andy Burnham and John Swinney discussed inter-government relationsByCraig WilliamsBBC ScotlandPrime Minister Andy Burnham has met the Scottish and Welsh first ministers ahead of the start of the 2026 Commonwealth Games.Burnham spoke to John Swinney and Rhun ap Iorwerth at a series of face-to-face meetings in Glasgow. Downing Street later said a second Scottish independence referendum was "off limits". Swinney said the new prime minister gave a commitment not to bypass Holyrood with his plans for further devolution of power, while ap Iorwerth said he set out the case for Wales to have parity of powers with Scotland.A Downing Street spokesperson said the prime minister congratulated Swinney and the city of Glasgow on hosting the 2026 Commonwealth Games. He said he was looking forward to the opening ceremony later in the evening.However, the cross-government meetings were dominated by conversations about the relations between London, Edinburgh and Cardiff.The Downing Street statement said: "The conversation then turned to how the prime minister and first minister can work more closely together to build a model of devolution to improve people's lives across Scotland."Burnham was described as speaking about his hope that devolution could help re-industrialise the UK, "creating jobs, transforming high streets and reviving local economies." He welcomed "the first minister's willingness to engage with that process".The spokesperson added: "The prime minister stated up front that another referendum on independence was off limits because it would take our focus away from growing the economy and helping families with the cost of living."More broadly, the prime minister said this was a moment for both sides to work together in a constructive relationship. He looked forward to regular engagement with the first minister in the months ahead."Meeting was 'very constructive' - SwinneySpeaking to BBC Scotland News afterwards, John Swinney said they had a "very constructive meeting" on areas "of common purpose" such as the cost of living and strengthening the economy."The prime minister is the first prime minister to have signalled that Westminster has failed many parts of the United Kingdom, including Scotland," he said."So there's welcome opportunities to advance discussions about where powers should lie within our country and that's what we've agreed to take forward."Swinney said Burnham's stated desire to devolve further powers around the UK was something he welcomed.Asked whether he was prepared to consider devolving powers out of the Scottish Parliament into local authorities or communities along the model Burnham is proposing in England, the first minister said: "The prime minister gave an important commitment today that the dialogue around future powers and how they might be devolved to local communities would be a discussion had with the Scottish government."It wouldn't be done over our heads and that's a really welcome assurance from the prime minister.'Parity' with Scotland call from Welsh FMImage source, ReutersImage caption, The new PM also met Welsh First Minister Rhun ap IorwerthThe two first ministers met Burnham separately on Thursday afternoon.Speaking afterwards, ap Iorwerth said he was pleased to meet the new prime minister and congratulated him on his new role."I welcomed the opportunity to begin a new chapter in the relationship between our respective governments, based on mutual respect and a shared commitment to delivering for the people we serve," he said."I know that Andy Burnham shares my belief in the power of devolution to change people's lives for the better. In that spirit, I set out the case for Wales to have parity of powers with Scotland through a new Wales Bill with an immediate emphasis on devolving policing and justice, and a fairer funding model to replace the outdated Barnett formula."He also said they had discussed the cost-of-living crisis and adopting "a more collaborative approach to politics"."I look forward to further positive and productive discussions as I continue to make the case for fairness and equality for Wales and all its communities," he said.

schema.headline: Burnham tells Swinney independence is 'off limits' at Glasgow Commonwealth Games meeting

title: Burnham's plan to collaborate with social media creators
description: Andy Burnham is a past master at using social media to create a political brand - and it seems he has no plans to let up now that he is prime minister
body: Andy Burnham is a past master at using social media to create a political brand - and it seems he has no plans to let up now that he is prime minister.One Downing Street source told the BBC they planned to take a "more social first" and "authentic to Andy" approach, with "less high production, polished" content than might have been seen from a prime minister in the past.This means we can expect more clips of Burnham in casual mode, chatting into a handheld phone or answering questions on policy in an apparently spontaneous fashion on X, Instagram or TikTok.There will also be a focus on collaborations with social media creators.In an earlier age, political leaders liked to court TV stars and other celebrities to help get their message out.Now the Holy Grail is online influencers, who are seen as more authentic, and trusted, by hard-to-reach voters who don't watch much TV or read newspapers.Sir Keir Starmer's government had already begun experimenting with giving access to influencers, with ministers appearing on some of the more popular channels.Downing Street also held receptions for influencers and during Tim Allan's tenure as director of communications content creators were invited to Downing Street press conferences.Some critics saw this is a way of avoiding serious scrutiny of polices in favour of softball questions from non-specialists. But Burnham's advisers see collaborations with influencers as a way of getting the government's message to audiences it might not normally reach.A Downing Street source said "the vast majority" of the public will not see or interact with content that is coming from government or the prime minister's social media channels.In the hours before and after becoming prime minister, Burnham co-authored two posts on TikTok and Instagram, including a running video with Sir Mo Farah.Young TikTokers were also invited to Downing Street to thank them for their work on his Makerfield by-election campaign.One of the social channels Sir Keir Starmer appeared on was run by Ukrainian online personality Max Klymenko, who won TikTok Creator of the year for his viral "The Career Ladder" series, where he quizzes people about their job history.Klymenko was first approached by Burnham's team around two months ago to collaborate on content.His team filmed the video quizzing the former Greater Manchester mayor on his first job, before he entered politics, a week ago, then planned to post it on his first day as prime minister to capitalise on the buzz.The creator's core audience are aged 18 to 24, according to his team, while the majority of people who watch his content are aged between 25 and 30.A member of his team pointed out this is far younger than the audience for a traditional TV interview, while his cumulative subscriber and follower count across TikTok, Instagram, YouTube and Facebook is more than 16.3 million accounts.While there is some paid-for content on his pages, Klymenko said no payment or contract was exchanged with Burnham's team and they did not request changes to the content before it was posted.Meanwhile, Sir Mo Farah's uploads, running while discussing sport, education and mental health with Burnham, went to his three million followers across YouTube, TikTok and Instagram, a far bigger reach than the prime minister's personal channels.The strategy is the brainchild of Abby Tomlinson, who ran Burnham's digital communications during the by-election, and who the BBC can confirm has been kept on for a new role in Burnham's administration.She began her career in social media aged 17 by starting a "Milifandom" campaign on social media for Ed Miliband over a decade ago.In more recent years, she has worked in digital communications for the Labour Party, and for Keir Starmer in Number10 before quitting last year.Tomlinson has been credited with how Burnham connected with voters across platforms in recent months.The new PM has rationed interviews with traditional media since returning to Parliament, in favour of vlogs, a question and answer session on Reddit and X posts clapping back at political opponents.Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch took to Reddit to urge him to "come out from hiding, face a proper press conference and submit yourself to scrutiny and some tough questions".Burnham responded to it by saying it had only been two weeks since he faced questions from "74,000 members of the public of Makerfield".Another key strand of Burnham's campaigning strategy has been connecting with and organising grassroots social media activists.Among the politicians and staffers invited to stand outside Number 10 to watch Burnham become prime minister on Monday were teenage TikTokers whose video edits have brought him to a new social media audience.Between them, Will, Lottie, Megan, Ellen and Poppy, all aged between 17 and 19  have racked up millions of likes on TikTok, and across platforms share memes, trends and nostalgic edits of New Labour footage, including of Burnham's time in office.The Labour activists were invited to canvass with him and make edits of his time on the Makerfield by-election campaign trail in a language and style that resonates with people online.One way they got thousands of likes was by getting him to sign and pose with red pre-made cocktails popular with Gen Z.Megan Cassidy, 19, from Havering, started making Labour content a year ago and said it was "surreal" to have found herself at Downing Street: "I really felt like I was witnessing history around me".The young Labour activists said they were given access to Burnham and footage of him by his team, but are not paid and can choose to edit the material how they like.They saw it as an opportunity to gain ground from social media savvy parties like Reform, whose leader Nigel Farage has an exponentially bigger following on social media, and the Greens who have experienced large growth in recent months.It helped that Burnham was "natural" and happy to spontaneously appear in content, they told the BBC."I do think he gets it, absolutely compared to other politicians, compared to Starmer for example, I think he's a lot more charismatic," said Megan.

schema.headline: Burnham's plan to collaborate with social media creators

title: Police failed to check Manchester synagogue attacker's phones despite arrests
description: Image source, FacebookImage caption, Jihad Al-Shamie, 35, was on bail for an alleged rape when he attacked the synagogue in ManchesterByEwan Gawne and
body: Image source, FacebookImage caption, Jihad Al-Shamie, 35, was on bail for an alleged rape when he attacked the synagogue in ManchesterByEwan Gawne and Rachael LazaroNorth WestUpdated 7 hours agoA police chief has apologised after admitting phones were twice seized from the Manchester synagogue attacker months before the car and knife assault but they were not examined.Jihad Al-Shamie, 35, was on bail for an alleged rape when he carried out the attack on Heaton Park Synagogue in Crumpsall on 2 October 2025, in which two Jewish men were killed.His devices, which contained evidence of his extreme views, were seized after he was arrested in February and September but not analysed.Greater Manchester Police Chief Constable Sir Stephen Watson said he was "deeply sorry", conceding that "the information contained within these devices would have inspired further investigative action".Sir Stephen said the end of legal proceedings against Mohammad Bashir, who has been jailed for life with a minimum term of 17 years for plotting a separate attack with Al-Shamie, meant rules around sharing information about a live criminal case had been lifted.He said: "In conducting an internal review, it became apparent that GMP had seized Al-Shamie's mobile devices, at the point of each arrest. "The significance of the data contents of these devices was substantial in respect of evidencing an extremist mindset."I am disappointed at the prospect of adding to the distress of all of those affected by the terrible events last October. "For this, I am deeply sorry."Figure caption, The moment the synagogue attacker and Bashir discuss becoming martyrsThe apology came after Bashir's sentencing over the plot with Al-Shamie to attack the UK's Defence Academy in Shrivenham. Details emerged in the case at Manchester Crown Court that the pair had been discussing their extremist views in WhatsApp chats, including preparing for a "war". The court was played footage showing the moment Al-Shamie and Bashir discussed becoming martyrs. Meanwhile, the Independent Office for Police Conduct (IOPC) confirmed it was investigating "all previous contact between Greater Manchester Police and Jihad Al-Shamie prior to the terror attack on 2 October 2025".A spokesperson for the police watchdog said this included the actions taken by police in response to reports of crimes committed by him.Image caption, Melvin Cravitz (left) and Adrian Daulby (right) died after Jihad Al-Shamie launched a car and knife attackOne officer is under investigation by the IOPC for potential misconduct in relation to their handling of allegations of rape and other sexual offences against Al-Shamie."This does not necessarily mean that disciplinary proceedings will follow. This decision will be made on conclusion of the investigation," the spokesperson said. Melvin Cravitz and Adrian Daulby were killed after Al-Shamie, from Prestwich, drove his Kia Picanto into the gates of the synagogue and began attacking people with a knife.Worshippers had gathered to mark Yom Kippur, the holiest day of the year for Jews.Al Shamie, who was wearing a fake suicide belt, was killed by police gunfire at the scene.Image source, PA MediaImage caption, Two worshippers were killed in the attack at the synagogue in Crumpsall The IOPC said its investigation would consider whether GMP missed any opportunities to prevent the attack. The watchdog said it was made aware of the information recovered from Al-Shamie's devices by GMP last week, and would review it as part of the investigation. Sir Stephen said Al-Shamie had been arrested on 13 February 2025 on suspicion of breaching a non-molestation order; and on the 21 September 2025 on suspicion of rape."Regrettably, I can confirm that the significance of the information stored on Al-Shamie's devices was not realised as they had not been examined before the attack took place on the 2 October 2025," he said.Sir Stephen said there was "little doubt" the extremist messages on the attackers phones would have led to further investigation, but the devices were not reviewed.The inquests into the deaths of Cravitz and Daulby in the synagogue attack are set to take place in spring 2027.The proposed scope of the inquests will include examining the radicalisation of Al-Shamie and whether the attack could have been prevented.Sir Stephen said those inquests would examine "all aspects of the attack".The chief constable added that it was for the IOPC and the coroner to determine the "impact" of police not reviewing the contents of Al-Shamie's devices before the attack took place.Get in touchTell us which stories we should cover in Greater Manchester

schema.headline: Police failed to check Manchester synagogue attacker's phones despite arrests

title: The BBC News app keeps you informed with live and breaking news you can trust
description: Depending on the contract you have, data charges may apply for accessing the internet on your mobile device.If you are not sure about the potential ch
body: Depending on the contract you have, data charges may apply for accessing the internet on your mobile device.If you are not sure about the potential charges, please ask your mobile network provider. You may find some costs are included in your existing price plan or that you can opt for a data package that gives reduced charges for accessing the internet.The BBC does not charge you to access mobile content.

schema.headline: The BBC News app keeps you informed with live and breaking news you can trust

title: Angela Rayner tells BBC she will not repay severance payment
description: A lump sum, equivalent of 25% of the annual ministerial salary, is payable when a minister stops holding office. Departing ministers who are aged over
body: A lump sum, equivalent of 25% of the annual ministerial salary, is payable when a minister stops holding office. Departing ministers who are aged over 65 or are reappointed within three weeks do not qualify.The 2025/26 accounts, external for the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government record the severance payment for Rayner.In her first interview since returning to government, Rayner was pressed on whether she would return the money.She told Matt Chorley on BBC Radio 5 Live: "No, my severance payment was given to me at the time, I had worked in government for a period of time and then I came out of government for just under a year."She added: "I will be delivering in government again."Rayner earlier said her criticism of the Conservatives over severance payments came at a time when there was a "revolving door" which saw ministers return to government "after 40-odd days".The Conservatives earlier this week said Rayner had "quit in disgrace and took a £17k payout" last year.The party added: "Now she's got her old job back, will she return the cash? Or is it one rule for Labour, and another for everyone else?"Rayner's departure last September came after the prime minister's ethics adviser, Sir Laurie Magnus, said she had "acted with integrity" but concluded that she had breached the ministerial code.Sir Laurie said she did get legal advice when buying the property, but failed to seek further expert tax advice as recommended.In May, Rayner resolved her tax affairs with HMRC following an investigation, settling £40,000 in unpaid stamp duty.The MP for Ashton-under-Lyne said she had been "exonerated" of the accusation that she had "deliberately sought to avoid tax".Rayner told Matt Chorley: "The situation I went through last year was really difficult, it was difficult for my family, but I felt I'd let people down and I never want to let people down, I want to fight for them."Rayner also said she has "unfinished business" in government, including on building council houses and supporting local government.Burnham has said he wants to deliver "the biggest council house building programme since the post-war period".Asked for details to explain what this will look like, Rayner said she would not provide an "exact figure" or provide a date for when she considers the post-war period to have ended.She said: "We want to aspire to get as far as possible and what we've done as a government is put more investment in that, the £39bn for social and affordable housing grant funding that we want to get out the door."

schema.headline: Angela Rayner tells BBC she will not repay severance payment

title: Molly Smith and Tom Clare: Love Island winners marry
description: Sharing more from their marriage ceremony, Molly posted a photo to her Instagram story of her hand on top of Tom's. The snap showed their wedding ring
body: Sharing more from their marriage ceremony, Molly posted a photo to her Instagram story of her hand on top of Tom's. The snap showed their wedding rings along with a bouquet of flowers, with the caption: "Husband and wife". The couple first got together two years ago, when they met on season one of Love Island: All Stars.All Stars is a spin-off of the hit dating show, which sees past contestants return for a second chance at finding love in the villa.During the reality dating show, the pair beat Molly's ex-boyfriend Callum Jones and his partner at the time, Jessica Gale, who finished as runners-up.Molly previously starred in series six in 2020 and left the show with Callum, who she was in a relationship with for three years before they split.While Hyde United footballer Tom previously came third place on the second winter edition of Love Island in 2023 with former partner Samie Elishi.Earlier this year, Tom and Molly took over hosting duties of the NearlyWeds podcast from radio presenters and reality stars Jamie Laing and Sophie Habboo.Fellow Love Island stars were among those to congratulate the couple, including former winner Casey O'Gorman who described it as the "best day". Sophie Piper, who came third on their All Star season, and TikTok star GK Barry also shared their support with the newly weds.

schema.headline: Molly Smith and Tom Clare: Love Island winners marry

title: Google and Tesla shares plunge as AI spending rattles markets
description: Shares of Google and Tesla plunged on Thursday as investors were spooked by the ever-increasing amounts of money being spent on artificial intelligenc
body: Shares of Google and Tesla plunged on Thursday as investors were spooked by the ever-increasing amounts of money being spent on artificial intelligence (AI).Google's parent company Alphabet saw its share price drop by nearly 7%, while Elon Musk's electric vehicle-maker Tesla saw its stock fall 14.5%.Both reported negative free cash flow - the money retained after paying for operations and investments - in financial results on Wednesday, alongside promises to spend billions more in the months and years to come.It was the first time Google had seen the cash metric turn negative since it became a public company in 2004, according to its financial records.As major tech companies race to capitalize on a new wave of AI technology, investors are wondering when financial benefits will show up. Alphabet now expects to spend as much as $205bn this year, mainly on AI projects and infrastructure, a $15bn increase from a spending estimate it gave just three months ago. Meanwhile, Tesla expects this year to spend up to $25bn on unspecified projects."There is still a healthy degree of scepticism about the ability of these investments to generate a commensurate level of return," Russ Mould, an investment director at AJ Bell, said. Alphabet's combined quarterly revenue hit $119.8bn, up 23% compared with the same period last year.Though Google parent Alphabet saw its business continue to grow in recent months, heavy spending on artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure pushed its leftover cash into negative territory.The company's free cash flow came in at negative $5.9bn (£4.3bn) for the first time in at least a decade, according to its past financial records.Its stock quickly fell 4% in after-hours trading.Anat Ashkanazi, Google's chief financial officer, noted on a call with financial analysts that the company had recorded negative free cash flow due to growing capital expenditures, essentially all of which were related to AI spending.She said the company spent $45bn in the second quarter, with 60% of the cost going towards servers and the remaining 40% going towards data centres. Alphabet's capital spending was $36bn in the first quarter of this year.Ashkanazi said on the call that when it comes to AI, "the demand still outpaces that investment". "As long as we see these attractive opportunities to invest, we will continue to invest."Sundar Pichai, Google's chief executive, said the technological shift to AI tools and capabilities still "feels like early innings in a shift across multiple areas" and the company's plans around generating financial returns on its spending were "disciplined". "What I see with what you can do with frontier capabilities, there is still a lot of work left to do to translate that into experiences for our users. So that looks like extraordinary opportunities with extraordinary returns."Rachel Winter, a partner at the wealth management firm Killik & Co, said there was some surprise among investors about how much Google was spending."These are huge numbers. The fact that the shares dropped when the results came out, that suggests there is a little bit of concern about those levels."Tesla reported negative free cash flow on Wednesday of $1.1bn for the second quarter due to its own increasing investment costs.It was the company's first negative showing of leftover cash in two years, according to its financial records.Tesla's plans to spend $25bn this year is more than double its capital spending in 2025.Vaibhav Taneja, Tesla's chief financial officer, said on Wednesday that Tesla was in "a big investment cycle" and its spending would probably increase further over the next three years.

schema.headline: Google and Tesla shares plunge as AI spending rattles markets

title: Aston Villa season preview: Can Unai Emery work magic again?
description: There was a sense at Villa Park last season they were getting into a party they were not invited to by qualifying for the Champions League. Villa gate
body: There was a sense at Villa Park last season they were getting into a party they were not invited to by qualifying for the Champions League. Villa gatecrashed the top five for the second time in three seasons, finishing fourth to comfortably secure entry to club football's elite competition for 2026-27.Emery has achieved this after inheriting a squad that was 14th in the table, three points above the relegation zone, in November 2022.Villa's revival has happened despite having to sell, with for example Jacob Ramsey joining Newcastle for £40m last summer, to ensure compliance with financial rules.Uefa's Squad Cost Ratio limits the amount clubs spend on transfers, wages and agent fees to 70% of their revenue. These financial shackles have long been a frustration for Villa. Selling Rogers was the easiest way to comply with the regulations but it means large-scale spending to replace the England forward as well as Tielemans and Digne is unlikely without further significant exits.Fringe players will leave though. Hull are keen on Leon Bailey but that may have to be an initial loan given the Tigers' own finances, while Evann Guessand can also depart. Tammy Abraham is however happy at Villa Park and not looking to leave.Villa would have preferred to keep Tielemans but could not afford to renegotiate his deal, which had two years left, before United triggered his £35m release clause.That is at least pure profit in the books after his free transfer arrival from Leicester City in 2023.With Tielemans and Rogers gone and with Digne poised to follow, Villa have lost 489 Premier League appearances from the squad - plus two of the players who scored in the Europa League final. Freiburg were poor that night in Istanbul but that did not reflect negatively on Johan Manzambi, who had caught Villa's eye more than a year before.He joined for a club record fee of more than £50m just two years after making his senior debut for Freiburg in what was a case of now or never. Villa were expecting the 20-year-old to move this summer - they hijacked a transfer to Newcastle - and had they not signed him, he would have been out of their reach in the future.Manzambi, crowned Europa League Young Player of the Year, represents the start of a new era along with Joao Gomes following his £38m move from Wolves.The Brazil midfielder made the third-highest number of tackles in the Premier League last season with 107 and his ball recoveries were the fourth highest in the division - winning possession 194 times. He adds crucial nous after Villa's plans were thrown a curveball following Amadou Onana's serious knee injury.Gomes is plug-and-play ready, with 116 top-flight appearances in his three-and-a-half years at Molineux. Chelsea's Nicolas Jackson could be an option with experience of the league as Villa look for a new striker, with the forward having worked with Emery at Villarreal - but the Blues' £65m price tag will be prohibitive. Emery knows he cannot afford players to take time to adapt to his meticulous methods so needs men he can trust quickly. AC Milan's former Brighton left-back Pervis Estupinan is therefore a candidate to bolster the defence.A new winger arrived in the form of Alejandro Garnacho from Chelsea and a right-back who can also play centrally is also on the wishlist.Last season, Villa's expected points was 48.50, which would have left them 12th - eight places below where they eventually finished. They were one of the clear over-achievers, with only Sunderland doing better, finishing seventh instead of an expected position of 18th. Villa did not drop out of the top four after beating Leeds last November, aided by winning 12 of 13 top-flight games between September and December. Then came the drop off, with just six league wins after the turn of the year as they needed to rely on the failings of others - notably Chelsea - to stay in the top five.Yet they managed it despite Boubacar Kamara's second-ending injury, John McGinn's knee problem that kept him out for 10 games and the knee issue for Tielemans that sidelined him for two months. During Tielemans' injury absence between February and April, Villa won just two of eight Premier League games - including a 4-1 defeat to Chelsea that left them on the brink of dropping out of the top four. Without him, Villa's Premier League win ratio dropped from 53% to 41%. It all adds to the quality of Emery's work, managing to keep Villa in the top four despite constant setbacks. Doing it again against a summer of change will be another test of the manager's skill.

schema.headline: Aston Villa season preview: Can Unai Emery work magic again?
